title: Sorting
description: Master C# sorting with Array.Sort, List.Sort, Comparison<T> delegates, and LINQ OrderBy for coding interviews.

sections.title: Array.Sort and List.Sort
sections.explanation: `Array.Sort(arr)` sorts an array in-place in ascending order. `list.Sort()` sorts a List<T> in-place. Both use introsort (O(n log n) average). To sort descending, sort ascending then `Array.Reverse(arr)`, or use a custom comparer. These are the go-to methods for interview problems. Note: both mutate the original collection.

exercise.prompt: Create an int array {7, 3, 9, 1, 5}. Sort it ascending and print joined by spaces. Then reverse it for descending and print again. Create a List<string> {"dog", "cat", "bird"}, sort it, and print joined by spaces.

validation.message: Ascending ints, descending ints, sorted strings.
Use Array.Sort for arrays, Array.Reverse for descending, and list.Sort() for lists.

exercise.hint: Array.Sort(nums) sorts in-place. Array.Reverse(nums) reverses in-place. words.Sort() sorts the list.

sections.title: Custom Sort with Comparison<T>
sections.explanation: Pass a `Comparison<T>` delegate to Sort for custom ordering. `Comparison<T>` is `(T a, T b) => int` where negative means a < b, zero means equal, positive means a > b. Use `a.CompareTo(b)` for ascending, `b.CompareTo(a)` for descending. For complex sorts (by length, by property), lambdas are concise: `list.Sort((a, b) => a.Length.CompareTo(b.Length))`.

exercise.prompt: Given a List<int> {-3, 1, -7, 4, 2, -5}, sort by absolute value ascending using a custom Comparison lambda. Print the sorted list. Then sort descending by absolute value and print again.

validation.message: Ascending by abs: 1,2,-3,4,-5,-7. Descending by abs: -7,-5,4,-3,2,1.
Use lambda comparison with Math.Abs for absolute value sorting.

exercise.hint: nums.Sort((a, b) => Math.Abs(a).CompareTo(Math.Abs(b))) for ascending. Swap a and b for descending.

sections.title: LINQ OrderBy and ThenBy
sections.explanation: LINQ provides non-mutating sort with `OrderBy()` and `OrderByDescending()`. Chain `ThenBy()` for secondary sorts. These return `IOrderedEnumerable<T>` — call `ToList()` or `ToArray()` to materialize. LINQ sort is more readable for multi-key sorts: `items.OrderBy(x => x.Category).ThenBy(x => x.Price)`. Import `System.Linq`.

exercise.prompt: Given string[] names = {"Charlie", "Alice", "Bob", "Dave", "Eve"}, use LINQ to sort by name length ascending, then alphabetically for same-length names. Print each name on its own line. Then print the names sorted descending by length using OrderByDescending.

validation.expected:
Bob
Eve
Dave
Alice
Charlie
Charlie
Alice
Dave
Bob
Eve
validation.message: First: sorted by length then alpha. Second: sorted by descending length.
Use LINQ OrderBy, ThenBy, and OrderByDescending.

exercise.hint: names.OrderBy(n => n.Length).ThenBy(n => n). Use ToArray() then iterate to print.

sections.title: Partial Sort and Top-K
sections.explanation: For top-K problems, you can sort and take first K (O(n log n)), or use a SortedSet/PriorityQueue for O(n log k). LINQ makes top-K clean: `nums.OrderByDescending(x => x).Take(k)`. For interview problems, sorting the full array is often acceptable. Array.Sort also supports sorting a range: `Array.Sort(arr, startIndex, length)`.

exercise.prompt: Given int[] scores = {88, 72, 95, 63, 91, 85, 77, 98, 70, 82}, find and print the top 3 scores (descending) and bottom 3 scores (ascending), each as a comma-separated list on its own line. Use LINQ.
exercise.hint: scores.OrderByDescending(x => x).Take(3) for top 3. OrderBy for bottom 3.
